coaching.coaching: Pin down FakeBackend contract suite verification criteria with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: FakeBackend contract suite verification criteria

coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance-criteria completeness and measurability will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance-criteria completeness and measurability

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on S5 irreversibility and rollback absence: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: S5 irreversibility and rollback absence

coaching.coaching: Propagate the revision for stale module-path references in developer documentation to the child tickets.
coaching.subject: stale module-path references in developer documentation

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify the epic-level unblocking AC to acknowledge that `BindingStore`'s `jira_key` coupling (a core module) is a remaining be74 prerequisite, so the 'no core edit' claim is scoped to field-mapping/transport/sanitization — not the binding identity layer — and update the proof-of-seam description accordingly.
evidence: Epic AC: 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface — verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit'
be74 epic description explicitly lists BindingStore as requiring generalization: '`jira_key` in the binding store (rebar_reconciler/binding_store.py) → a {provider, external_key} abstraction'
BindingStore class docstring: 'Bidirectional local-id ↔ jira-key binding store' — core module, not under adapters/jira/
FakeBackend is test-only and can paper over BindingStore's jira_key coupling in tests, making the contract suite pass without exposing the real-backend gap
findings.finding: The epic AC states 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface — verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit.' However, `BindingStore` — a core module — remains a `local_id ↔ jira_key` (vendor-keyed) store even after all five stories. The be74 epic explicitly calls this out as requiring generalization ('`jira_key` in the binding store → a `{provider, external_key}` abstraction'). So the Phase-2 end-state does not actually satisfy 'no core edit' for a real second backend, only for the test-only FakeBackend.
findings.location: Epic AC bullet 7 ('Epic be74 is unblocked'); be74 epic description ('jira_key in the binding store'); BindingStore class in binding_store.py
scenarios: Implementer of be74 (GitHub Issues backend) discovers that BindingStore.get_jira_key / bind_confirm / record_pending_key are woven through reconcile.py, dispatch_one.py, run_differs.py, etc. — requiring core edits beyond 'only adapters/<x>/'
The proof-of-seam claim is verified by FakeBackend (which doesn't exercise BindingStore's jira_key coupling) and declared PASS, giving false confidence that be74 needs no core edits
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the epic AC bullet 7 to: 'Epic be74's field-mapping/transport seam is unblocked; BindingStore generalization (jira_key → RemoteRef) remains a be74 prerequisite tracked in that epic — the FakeBackend contract suite verifies the mapper/transport/sanitizer seam only.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan for S5's no-shims module relocation (e.g., a short-lived re-export shim window, a revert-commit strategy, or a grep-gated import audit step that confirms zero non-test references to the old paths before the shims are dropped).
evidence: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/ ... no shims'
'atomically (no shims)' — explicit rejection of expand-contract sequencing
Absence of any rollback plan, back-out commit strategy, or temporary re-export window in any story or AC
findings.finding: S5's physical module relocation ('no shims') is a destructive, hard-to-reverse step, but the plan specifies no rollback or back-out procedure. The 'green suite at every landed change' gate is a forward quality gate, not a recovery mechanism. If S5 lands and a latent issue surfaces post-merge (e.g., a runtime import path used outside the test suite, a downstream tool that imports the old paths), there is no described path to revert or temporarily re-export from the old location.
findings.location: S5 description and AC bullet: 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims'
scenarios: A downstream script or integration test outside the tracked ~29 patch strings imports the old module path; failure is discovered after S5 merges with no quick back-out available.
A CI environment caches the old bytecode paths; the 'green suite' gate passes locally but fails in a deployment environment with no described recovery.
findings.suggested_fix: Before S5 merges, add a pre-merge audit step: `grep -r 'from rebar_reconciler.acli\|from rebar_reconciler.adf\|from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields\|from rebar_reconciler.comment_limits' --include='*.py' .` must return zero hits outside the adapter layer. Document this as the back-out gate. Alternatively, land S5 with temporary re-export shims at the old paths, run one full release cycle, then drop them in a follow-up commit — classic expand-contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a doc-audit step to S5: scan ADR 0035 and any developer-facing docs for references to the old module paths (acli*, adf, outbound_fields, comment_limits at the package root) and update or annotate them to reflect the new adapters/jira/ locations.
evidence: 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed' — code references handled, doc references not mentioned
Absence of any doc-audit or stale-reference-check step in S5 or its AC
ADR 0035 itself may reference the old module layout established in Phase 1
findings.finding: After S5 physically relocates modules, any existing developer documentation, onboarding guides, or inline README references that cite the old module paths (e.g., `rebar_reconciler/acli*`, `rebar_reconciler/adf.py`, `rebar_reconciler/outbound_fields.py`) will be silently stranded. The plan addresses code-level repointing of `inbound_fields.py` and `outbound_comments.py` but says nothing about auditing or updating doc-level references to the relocated files.
findings.location: S5 description: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/'
scenarios: A new contributor follows the onboarding doc, tries to find `rebar_reconciler/adf.py` per the documented path, and cannot — silent confusion with no plan update.
ADR 0035's Phase 1 section (which documented `jira_fields.py` moving to adapters/jira/) may now contain stale layout diagrams or file-path examples that conflict with the S5 outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the neutrality-gate mechanism in the S4 AC (e.g., 'enforced by an `import-linter` rule in `.importlinter` blocking `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler` → `adapters.jira.*`') so the executor knows exactly what artifact to produce and CI knows what to run.
evidence: 'After S4 the backend-neutral core no longer imports adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules — enforced by a neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites.'
The plan names no tool, no config file, no command that implements the gate; 'neutrality gate' is used as if self-defining.
F1 impact: without a specified gate mechanism, 'enforced' is not an observable outcome — it is an intent statement.
findings.finding: The 'neutrality gate' on the 10 differ/apply sites (S4 AC) is unspecified: the plan says the core's absence of adapters.jira.* imports is 'enforced by a neutrality gate' but never states what that gate is (import-linter rule, custom CI script, pytest conftest fixture, Makefile check, etc.). An executor cannot implement 'enforced' without deciding this, and the wrong choice (e.g., a comment rather than a CI-blocking check) would silently fail the criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 3: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites'
scenarios: An executor implements the rewiring but omits a CI check; a future PR re-introduces a vendor import and the gate is silent.
Two executors independently choose different gate mechanisms (import-linter vs. grep vs. pytest) creating inconsistent enforcement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the S4 AC: 'Gate is implemented as an import-linter contract (`.importlinter`) that is run by `make lint`; a failing import causes a CI red.' Adjust the tool name to match whatever import-checking tool the project already uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the file-location loader in the plan (e.g., cite the module/function responsible and what change is required) so S5 is self-contained without codebase archaeology.
evidence: 'teach the file-location loader the new dir' — S5 description
'the loader resolves the location-pinned modules there' — AC line 4
No section of the plan explains what the file-location loader is, where it lives, or how it is configured.
findings.finding: The 'file-location loader' referenced in S5 ('teach the file-location loader the new dir') is never defined anywhere in the plan. An executor unfamiliar with this loader mechanism cannot know what file to edit, what API to call, or how to verify the loader resolves the relocated modules correctly.
findings.location: Child decomposition S5 and AC line 4: 'the loader resolves the location-pinned modules there'
scenarios: An executor relocates the files but misses the loader update, producing silent import failures at runtime that are not caught until integration tests run against a real Jira instance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe the concrete artifact that constitutes the 'neutrality gate' in S4 (e.g., a pytest import-boundary test, a ruff/flake8 rule, or a CI script) so implementers know what to build and reviewers know what to inspect.
evidence: AC text: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites'
No corresponding description in S4's decomposition entry ('Changes callers, not file locations') or in the Design section.
The decomposition entry for S3 mentions the registry but says nothing about import enforcement.
findings.finding: The AC for S4 references a 'neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites' as the enforcement mechanism, but no section of the plan describes what artifact constitutes this gate (e.g., an import-guard test, a CI lint rule, a module-level __all__ restriction). An implementer cannot build something that isn't specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S4 bullet: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites'
scenarios: Implementer completes S4 caller rewiring but omits the gate entirely, leaving the constraint unenforced in CI.
Reviewer cannot verify the AC because the artifact to inspect is unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the S4 decomposition entry, e.g.: 'Implement the neutrality gate as a pytest test (tests/architecture/test_core_neutrality.py) that asserts no module under rebar_reconciler/ imports from adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules; this test is added in S4 and must remain green in S5.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the S4 AC) that explicitly verifies the hardcoded '"jira"' string literal is absent from the backend-neutral core after S4, replaced by RemoteRef usage — covering both removal and replacement per migration-criteria best practice.
evidence: Design: 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal'
AC bullet 1 only verifies RemoteRef exists and the contract suite passes — not that the literal is gone.
AC bullet 2 verifies the default ('jira') reproduces behavior — this is about the config string value, not the removal of hardcoded literals in core logic.
No AC bullet states 'the hardcoded string literal "jira" no longer appears in core differ/apply code'.
findings.finding: The Design section states 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal', describing a concrete replacement of a literal string throughout the codebase. However, no AC bullet verifies that the hardcoded 'jira' literal has been removed — only that RemoteRef exists (S2 AC) and that the default config value reproduces behavior (S3 AC).
findings.location: Design section — 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal'; Acceptance Criteria bullets 1 and 2
scenarios: S2 adds RemoteRef as a new type but the hardcoded literals remain in core code; all ACs pass but the design goal is unmet.
A future backend implementation collides with the unremoved literal, requiring an unplanned core edit — exactly what epic be74 was supposed to avoid.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in S4 whether `apply_inbound.py` and `apply_planning.py` (which also call `_jira_key_to_local_id` and contain hardcoded `jira-` prefix checks) are in scope for the neutrality rewire, or explicitly document them as deferred — and update the neutrality gate to cover all core modules, not just the 10 listed differ/apply sites.
evidence: S4 AC: 'None of the 10 differ/apply sites import adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules' — the gate is scoped to the 10 named sites only
apply_inbound.py lines 78, 151, 194, 222, 290 call _jira_key_to_local_id (from inbound_translate.py) — a Jira-specific local-id derivation — and apply_planning.py line 129 does the same
apply_inbound.py also has 'target.startswith("jira-")' checks on lines 151, 194, 290 — hardcoded Jira local-id shape checks
Neither apply_inbound.py nor apply_planning.py appear in the ADR §(a) 10-site list or the S4 file_impact list
Epic AC: 'after S4 the backend-neutral core no longer imports adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules — enforced by a neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites'
findings.finding: The S4 neutrality gate (AC: 'backend-neutral core no longer imports adapters.jira.* or root vendor modules') covers only the 10 enumerated differ/apply sites, but `apply_inbound.py` (lines 78, 151, 194, 222, 290) and `apply_planning.py` (line 129) also call `_jira_key_to_local_id` from `inbound_translate.py` — a Jira-coupled function — and are not listed in the S4 edit-set or the neutrality grep/test gate. If the neutrality gate is run only against the 10 named files, it will pass green while Jira-coupling persists in two other core modules.
findings.location: S4 story (6673-7636-a116-4f90) AC bullet 1; epic AC bullet 3; ADR 0035 §(a) leak-site list
scenarios: Implementer runs the S4 neutrality grep on the 10 named modules only, gets zero hits, marks AC green — but apply_inbound.py still contains _jira_key_to_local_id and jira- prefix checks that couple the core to the Jira identity convention
A second backend implementer in epic be74 finds apply_inbound.py still hardcodes jira- prefix derivation even after Phase 2 is 'complete'
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `apply_inbound.py` and `apply_planning.py` to the S4 edit-set with `_jira_key_to_local_id` replaced by an `IdentityConvention.remote_to_local_id(RemoteRef)` call, OR add an explicit deferral note in S4 AC and the epic-level AC that these two modules remain Jira-coupled until be74 generalizes `IdentityConvention` further.

findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.finding: S4 AC2's grep-based verification that ADF-sizing/lossy-status/value-maps 'live only under adapters/jira/' is ambiguous before S5: the root files (adf.py, outbound_fields.py) still physically exist after S4 and may still contain the original implementations, making the grep return hits at both root and adapters/jira/ paths until S5 completes the move.
findings.suggested_fix: Reword S4 AC2 to: 'ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping, and the duplicated value-maps/link constants are only invoked via the Backend port in core modules (grep for direct calls returns zero hits in the 10 sites); the Jira-specific implementations reside in adapters/jira/ files. Physical de-duplication of root-file copies is completed in S5.' This separates the call-site neutrality (verifiable at S4) from the physical single-sourcing (verifiable at S5).

findings.finding: After S5 physically moves adf.py and comment_limits.py into adapters/jira/, two non-moved root modules (inbound_fields.py and outbound_comments.py) will have broken hardcoded sibling-relative filename references that are NOT in S5's file_impact list and will not be caught by the plan's 'grep for old module paths' structural gate (which targets dotted-import strings, not lazy_load filename arguments).
findings.location: S5 (dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9) — Edit-set / Scope / Acceptance Criteria; also src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py:36, outbound_comments.py:84,100
findings.suggested_fix: In S5's edit-set, explicitly enumerate all callers of `lazy_load(key, 'adf.py')` and `lazy_load(key, 'comment_limits.py')` and `Path(__file__).parent / 'adf.py'` across src/ (not just the moved files), update each to resolve under `adapters/jira/` instead of the package root, and add those files to file_impact. Extend the structural gate to: `grep -rn "'adf.py'\|'comment_limits.py'" src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/ | grep -v adapters/jira/` expecting zero hits after the move.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
